sentences.text: Yes.
The horse meat, of course, was prepared like any other meat, was either fried or baked.
But what-- when there was no horse meat anymore, there were several horses elsewhere and people collected blood, horse blood.
My mother, I recalled watching, used to throw the blood into boiling water, which coagulated and looked like liver, and then sliced and baked or fried the liver.
It was tasting like a liver.
There was a shortage of food.
I mean, the shortage was no food, because stores had no food.
But bakeries opened and there were long lines.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were incidents where Polish anti-Semites, when they saw Jews standing in line for bread, used to throw them out, push them out, in the beginning.
I had no problem.
I don't know if I had no specifically Semitic looks.
I spoke fluent Polish, perfect as-- good or better than anyone else.
And-- but I've seen that happen.
No.
No.
No.
It was ration.
You can only get a portion, I think was a pound or something of that sort, of bread, small bread.
That's all you could get.
And you stayed very, very long.
Very long lines, very long in the line to get it.
I don't recall any other.
I think that we had leftover of cereal, the Polish kasha cereal.
Grits.
I don't know what's the exact translation in English.
But there was very, very little food at the time.
I think myself, my sister, my brother, not-- to my knowledge, not my parents.
They were-- they were afraid and concerned because of the anti-Semitic outbreaks that the hooligans, the Polish hooligans, would start.
The-- I recall the cavalry unit.
There was only few.
There was no more than maybe a dozen soldiers and four or five horses.
They-- the horses were staying outside in a garden that was part of the apartment complex.
And the soldiers were in a building that housed before the war a Hebrew high school, gymnasium, called [HEBREW].
Yes.
Not my immediate family, but my-- one of my cousins, Michal Gold, was a student in that-- in that school.
No.
About-- of course, when the war started, there was no school.
And after the German occupied Warsaw, my father decided that because of the potential of the Nazi or German persecution of Jewish men, basically that we should leave Warsaw.
Also, we found ourselves without any financial means.
The banks were closed.
So he thought that perhaps he could recover something from his factory in the eastern part of Poland, not knowing that it'll be occupied by the Soviets.
So my father, my brother, and I left Warsaw-- About what month would you say this was.
I would say that was no more than a-- it was probably in late October, 1939, no more than a month after the German occupied Warsaw.
I can't recall.
I can't-- I know that we had a very, very emotional goodbyes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look what she did.
Get her another-- Claire, she drank from that glass.
Yes.
Yes.
I would say, the best of my recollection, that it took us at least a week.
We went-- we walked.
We had backpacks.
We used farmers' or peasants' carts.
The German army was moving almost the same direction we were moving, moving east.
I mean, they already occupied it, but they were-- on the highway there were the German tanks and German trucks, military equipment.
And we were sometimes walking and sometimes going with peasant carts-- I don't know if my father paid them or gave them-- I don't recall that-- until we came to the River Bug.
That's B as in boy, U-G.
The river flew-- flows from south to north.
When we crossed the river, we found ourself under the Soviets.
The Soviet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occupied that part.

There was an agreement prior to the war between Germany and Soviet Union, non-aggression agreement called the Ribbentrop-Molotov agreement, that when Germany invades Poland, it'll divide Poland between the Soviet Union and Germany, the River Bug being the border of it.
So the eastern part of Poland, the Polish Ukraine, the Polish Lithuania part, and the Polish Belarus would fall into the Soviet Union.
No.
Not at all.
No.
We learned about it after.
We had no idea that those territories would be the Soviet-occupied.

In fact, when my father wanted to go to the town where the factory was, once we crossed the border and found the Soviets occupying that part, he immediately changed his mind, because he was in a Soviet eyes the enemy of the-- of the Soviet regime, being a capitalist.
So he made a decision to move to a-- another town, Bialystok.
There was-- there was no-- no-- at that time, we were possibly one of the first that crossed.
There was no reaction at all.
I have learned later from others that many were arrested by the Soviets.
In fact, many of ex-Polish Communists that took the opportunity, once they learned the Soviets are there, to run on the other side were immediately arrested.
The Soviet considered them greater enemies, because they were ideologists.
And when they saw the Soviet system, they were certainly not accepting that as a Marxist.
So they arrested them first.
The Communists and the Socialists were arrested immediately.
But they also arrested people just crossing later, but not in our group.
My father rented a room.
We had one room.
My father found work in Soviet food producing enterprise-- I don't recall it-- in fruits and vegetables.
But I don't recall the name.
But I and my brother went to the local school, former Polish schools.
Russian and I took Latin also in the school.
There was-- it was not compulsory, but I took it because my sister used to have Latin.
And that's all we had, just the Russian and Polish.
The school was Polish school, but it had a compulsory Russian language.
Shortly after we established ourself in Bialystok, a order came out that all residents and non-residents must register for internal passports.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The non-residents, what-- the refugees, so to say, that came from west like us, received a passport with so-called 11th paragraph. 11 paragraph was a restrictive passport.
Every Soviet citizen carried a passport everywhere in Soviet Union.
The 11 paragraph restrict that you were not allowed to live, I don't know exactly, but X amount of miles from a border, any border.
In certain big cities you are not allowed to travel.
It was a very restrictive one.
Shortly after, there was an order that all people with 11 paragraph should [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] come to a given place.
They were arrested, taken by the Soviets, and shipped out.
Some of them, they were shipped to Siberia, some south to Kazakhstan, to the deserts of Kazakhstan.
When that happened, we hid.
My father, my brother, and I, we hid in houses.
We hid in a barn until this thing was over.
And then we moved out.
We got out of Bialystok and moved to Ukraine, south.
Of course, I wish that they would have captured us and sent us to Siberia.
Perhaps my father and brother would have survived.
No.
No.
Never. no.
That was very dangerous, for him to be revealed as a capitalist.
No.
We lost total contact, direct contact, with our family in Warsaw throughout the stay with the Soviets.
The only thing that we heard, rumors what's happening in Warsaw.

The rumors, of course, at a later time, that a ghetto was established, that prosecution-- that the Germans are killing people, they are forcing them into labor, that there is no food, that-- all the negative things that started, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that Warsaw ghetto is getting very crowded.
But that's only rumors that came-- people that run away from there.
And we are very concerned, of course, what's happening to my mother and sister, but we had no news.
It was not an interior Russia.
It was former Polish Ukraine.
It was also the occupied territories.
By the way, the Soviet never occupied any territories.
They call it liberated territories.
It was Ukraine.
The-- the town was named Krzemieniec.
That's K-R-Z-E-M-- N as Nancy, --I-E-C.
Oh, it was much smaller town, very small town.
I would say the town had no more than 10,000 inhabitants.
It was a famous Polish town, because one of great Polish writers, Czacki, was born there.
No.
I can't recall it.
I can't recall the titles.
I read him, but I can't recall the titles.
He was a author.
It was in-- it was in 1940 that we got there, in the beginning.
And I think it was either February or March of 1940.
We again-- we rented a room in the house of a Jewish family that lived there.
We all three stayed in the one room.
We ate-- my father paid.
We ate dinners and whatever food with the family, whatever the woman, the wife of the owner-- I don't recall their names-- cooked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that I was doing the repairs.
I used to repair of socks and so, because I learned that in school.
I was the housewife.
I went to school, the local school.
So did my brother.
And my father found a job again in a food-related industry there.
In fact, what it was was a pickle and sauerkraut producing plant.
And he became later a manager there.
No.
But we were taught by my father to be very careful, very cautious.
We were questions in school, who are your parents?
Where you come from?
And we told that my father, my grandfather were workers, poor workers in Poland, repeat that over and over again.
So they did not bother us after-- after that.
No-- no-- no other contact, to my knowledge, except the family we lived in.
Maybe my father had one.
I have no recollection of it.
Yes.
It was a-- a good school.
Again, that school, we not only had to learn Russian, but we also had learn Ukrainian besides.
It was a Polish school.
But we had to learn Russian literature, Russian language, grammar, and Ukrainian literature and Ukrainian language.
The other subject-- there was great emphasis in Soviet Union on sciences.
We started getting physics already.
Again, the classes where you do handiwork, and I remember building in a class a small steam engine from parts.
Yes.
But he was higher class.
He was only-- either he quit the school or he finished, because he started working.
I also work, after school and during weekends, vacations.
By the way, there is no weekends-- there were no weekends in Soviet Union.
They-- you work five days and the sixth day was free.
Five days and sixth days was free, which is a genius idea not allowing two people to meet, because your sixth day not necessarily falls the same sixth day as other.
It keeps changing, so that prevents meeting of people and trying to overthrow the government or something of that sort.
We-- I worked in a-- putting crates, nailing crates together.
And it was a piecework.
You had to nail some X amount of crates, you know.
And you earned the-- the Russian money.
And I recall at one time, every-- every week we had a political commissar.
After work, we had to go to a meeting.
After school, after work, we had to listen to the political propaganda.
And one time the commissar came out.
And he says, listen.
We have a great idea.
We will donate one week of our work to the glory of the Red Army and to the honor of our leader, Comrade Stalin.
Who is against?
And that was-- that's-- that's typical with them.
They say, who is against?
And of course, if you cough, you wound up in Siberia.
So we work one week for nothing for the glory of the Red Army.
During this period of time, what did you-- you were talking we're in 1940.
What did you know of Hitler's expansion?
Very little.
The Soviet papers, the war, of course, in France, attack on France, we had very little news, except that France capitulated after two weeks of war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some articles, very short one, in the newspapers, the Soviet newspapers about the sea war, between Germans, that the ships were sunk.
You know, there were two major papers in Soviet Union, one called which is 'truth' in Russian.
The other is which is 'news.' And the joke went around that there is no truth in the news and no news in the truth, which was a fact.
Mostly, the paper were used either as toilet paper, because we're full of propaganda, or as to roll up cigarettes.
The Russians, the soldiers, used to use the cheapest tobacco called machorka, M-A-C-H-O-R-K, would roll it up into newspapers inside things and smoke it.
They were all of glory of Stalin and glory of Soviet labor and what a tremendous progress and what a horrible life the capitalists live and all that stuff, and nobody read it.
Yes.
The attack came in June, 1941.
It was unexpected.
It was not only unexpected to us.
It supposedly was unexpected to Stalin himself, even though he got prior news-- I found out later-- that there will be an attack.
Our town was taken over within three days.
It was very unexpected.
Just like that it happened and the German army marched in.
OK.

The day following the German entry to our town, the Ukrainian youth formed gangs, went house to house, and picked up Jews, whoever they could get and [INAUDIBLE] run them to the local jail, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they beat them to death with chains, with wood sticks, with whatever they could get, while they were supposed to dig with their hands, with their nails, graves of victims of Soviet [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rule, where the Soviets killed prisoners supposedly, Ukrainian or whoever.
And they murdered them.
We were hidden, my father, my brother, and I.
But it was quiet, so I went out to look what's happening.
And they got me.
They grabbed me.
There were four boys.
One of them went to the same class with me to school.
Ukrainian boys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The others were much older.
And they make me run and they kept telling, the Ukrainians, we're going to kill you Jews.
They ran me toward the jails.
About halfway down the road, a group of Ukrainians coming back from the jail-- evidently delivered their-- their people, the Jewish people there-- stop and started talking.
While they stopped, started talking, I jumped out.
I ran.
I jumped to a fence, one fence.
Then there was a very tall fence, maybe six feet tall or so.
I went right over it into a potato field which was full grown.
And I laid down in the potato field.
A potato field grown is very high.
It's about four, five-- four feet at least.
There was a [INAUDIBLE] and there was a woman on the porch who saw me.
But when the Ukrainians ran after me and they asked her, did you see-- I could hear them.
Did you see the Jewish boy, the Jew?
Where is he?
And she said, I didn't see anything.
They-- running through the potato fields, they spent at least as I recall a couple hours going all over.
They passed me by like 10 feet away, couldn't see me.
They didn't find me.
After it got dark-- I stayed there in a potato-- laid there 'til it got dark.
Then I went to a-- to a barn.
I found a barn and stayed in a barn 'til next morning, 'til the morning.

In the morning, I saw wagons, horse wagons, full of dead bodies from the jail, running from the jail, the blood dripping from the-- from the carriages of the people that they murdered at the-- in the jail.
I came home and my father and brother were there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was then that my father started talking that we better get back to Warsaw-- we under the Germans anyway-- and find a way to get back to Warsaw.

It was in January, 1942 that he got a guide, a fellow that would-- by the way, prior to that, we already were forced to wear yellow patches, not stars, just yellow round patch on the back of you-- of your jacket and on the left side, breast side.
The ghetto was still not-- not formed.

But he obtained the services of a guide who would guide us over the border between the territories that the German originally occupied, called the General Government, and the territories of the Soviet that they now occupied, which they called the Eastern-- Eastern Territories, Ostgebieten.
In January, at night we left the town.
We tore off these patches, the yellow patches, and walk to the nearest-- the next town railroad station, where we were supposed to gather with the guide, take a train to the city of Lvov.
That was a major city in Poland, and of course in Ukraine, the town.
Lvov is L-V-O-V.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, someone recognized us, denounced us, and the Gestapo came by sled-- was snow all over-- and arrested us, brought us to the Gestapo headquarters, which was a house just, and questioned us and beat us around for several hours, then brought us back to the jail, the same jail that the pogrom went on.
And we were put in a cell where there were about 40 people together, very small.
The cell was I think no bigger than this room, which is what? 30-something by 20.
There was not enough space to lay down.
We had to lay down in-- in-- what's the words?
Changes.
In shifts.
In shifts.
And stood most of the time.
There was no beds there was nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had--
Yes.
We get one soup a day, thin soup a day.
We had to work all day, kneeling on a wood floor and with bottles-- with the edge of a bottle pressed against the floor.
And if you do that, you create a shine.
You call it shining.
You can-- instead of using wax.
You take this and you do this, like that with the edge. you press it.
And that was the work.
We were in a cell with a French teacher.
Of course, people were-- were depressed that some of them didn't know why they were arrested.
They were just arrested on the street.
Didn't know what's going to happen to us.
